actions.thought: I am in the Button/Small Blind position in a heads-up match with QJs, which is a premium hand in this format (top 10-15% of hands). With 100BB stacks, the standard play is to raise to seize the initiative and build the pot. A min-raise to 200 allows me to play a wide range profitably and keeps the pot manageable while in position.

I am in the Big Blind with Q3o. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. While the price to call is only 100 more (getting 3:1 odds), Q3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially when out of position. In a long-form match, I prefer folding the bottom of my range to minimize difficult spots early on.

In a heads-up match, King-Seven offsuit (K7o) is a strong hand from the button. It is well within a standard opening range (top 80%). I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take the initiative, put pressure on the Big Blind, and potentially take down the pot immediately or build it with a hand that holds significant equity against the opponent's defending range.

In a heads-up scenario, Q6 suited is a defensible hand from the Big Blind against a Button open. The opponent raised to 3bb (300). I need to call 200 into a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% equity. Q6s has sufficient equity against a wide heads-up opening range to justify a call. 3-betting is an option but calling keeps the pot manageable while playing out of position. Folding would be too tight given the suited connector potential and high card strength relative to a wide range.

On this Ace-high flop (Ac 4c 5d), the pre-flop raiser (Flash) has a significant range advantage. As the Big Blind defender, I am out of position with Queen-high, a backdoor flush draw, and a backdoor straight draw (4-5-6). Leading out (donk betting) is not strategic here as it folds out bluffs and builds a pot against a stronger range. The standard play is to check to the aggressor and re-evaluate based on whether they bet or check back.

The flop 4c Ac 5d is a strong board for my range as the pre-flop raiser. An Ace-high board is difficult for the Big Blind to defend against if they didn't hit an Ace or a piece of the low straight/pair. I have the King of Clubs, providing a backdoor flush draw. I will place a continuation bet of 200 (1/3rd of the pot) to exert pressure and likely take the pot down immediately.

On an Ace-high flop (Ac 4c 5d), my hand (Queen-high) has very little immediate showdown value. Although I have a backdoor flush draw (diamonds) and a backdoor straight draw (4-5-6), playing this out of position against a range that connects well with the Ace is difficult. The opponent bet 1/3 pot (200 into 600), which is a standard size for their entire range, including strong Ax hands. While the pot odds (20%) are tempting, realizing equity with Q-high out of position is challenging without a stronger draw or pair. Floating here often leads to folding on the turn or bleeding chips. Folding is the disciplined, low-variance play early in the match.

I hold King-Jack offsuit (Kh Jd) on the Button (Small Blind). In heads-up play, this is a very strong starting hand. Being in position allows me to control the pot size and exert pressure. I will open with a raise to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A minimum raise to 200 is a standard size that balances risk and reward effectively.

With K3o in the Big Blind, I am getting 3:1 odds to call a single raise from the Button. While K3o is not a premium hand, it has enough high-card value to defend in a heads-up situation. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without over-committing out of position.

I am out of position as the Big Blind on a Queen-high flop that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. My hand is currently just King-high with no direct draw. Checking is the most standard and prudent play here to see how the opponent proceeds before committing more chips.

I hold Middle Pair (Jacks) with a King kicker on a Q-high board (Qs Jh 6d). While I have showdown value, my hand is vulnerable to Qx hands which are well within the Big Blind's defending range. Betting now likely only gets called by better hands (Queens, Two Pair) or strong draws (KT, T9), while folding out hands I dominate. Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, avoid getting check-raised, and induce bluffs on later streets from weaker hands. It is a standard way to navigate middle pair in position on a wet board.

The flop was checked through after Pro raised pre-flop, which often indicates a capped range or a desire to control the pot size with a marginal hand. The 4c on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. With King-high, I have very little showdown value, but betting here into a range that likely contains Jacks or small pairs might not be effective. I will check to see how Pro responds; if the turn is checked through again, I might have a chance to win at showdown or consider a river bluff depending on the final card.

I hold Second Pair (Jacks) with a King kicker on a board of Jh Qs 6d 4c. My opponent has checked to me twice, indicating weakness or a draw, though they could be trapping with a strong hand or holding a weak Queen. Betting here is thin; worse hands like King-high or small pocket pairs likely fold, while better hands (Qx, Sets, Two Pair) call or raise. Checking back is the best option for pot control. It prevents getting blown off my equity by a check-raise, keeps the pot small for a marginal hand, and allows me to potentially induce bluffs on the river or call a reasonable bet. The 4c changes nothing, so the plan to get to showdown cheaply remains valid.

The board has been checked through on the flop and turn, suggesting that neither player has a particularly strong hand. With King-high (K-3o), I have a small amount of showdown value in a checked-down pot, as I beat many of the opponent's possible air hands or missed straight draws. Betting here would likely only get called by hands that beat me (like a Jack, a Six, or Ace-high) and fold out hands I already beat. Checking is the most logical play to reach a cheap showdown and potentially win the pot.

I hold Second Pair (Jacks) with a King kicker on a board of Jh Qs 6d 4c 2d. The opponent has checked to me on the Flop, Turn, and River. This line heavily suggests a weak hand (like a weak pair, missed draw, or Ace-high) or possibly a slow-played monster/top pair, though the latter is less likely given the lack of value betting on three streets. Betting here is thin; most worse hands (Ace-high, smaller pairs like 4x or 2x) will fold, and better hands (Qx) will call. The only hands I might get value from are stubborn mid-pairs like 6x or 88-TT, but the risk of being check-raised or value-owning myself against a slow-played Queen outweighs the potential gain of a small bet. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity and win the pot against their bluffs and weaker made hands without risking more chips.

I hold Queen-Jack offsuit on the button, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates a raise here to build the pot and take initiative. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard sizing that applies pressure to the big blind while staying balanced with my overall opening range.

Opponent raises to 300 (3BB) from the Button. This 3x sizing offers worse pot odds (2:1 or 33% equity required) compared to a standard min-raise. While K4o has decent raw equity against a wide heads-up opening range, it suffers from poor playability out of position (unsuited, weak kicker, disconnected). Against a 3BB open, defending ranges should tighten, and K4o usually falls below the defending threshold for offsuit Kings (often K8o+ or K5o+ depending on the model). Defending marginal offsuit hands OOP against larger sizings is a common leak; therefore, a disciplined fold is the best long-term play.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with King-Seven offsuit (K7o). In Heads-Up play, this is a respectable hand that benefits significantly from being in position post-flop. With effective stacks at roughly 100 big blinds, a standard strategy is to open-raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and define the pot. Folding is too passive for a King-high hand in position, and while limping is an option, raising to 200 (a minimum raise) is the standard play to assert initiative.

I am holding 8s 6s in the big blind. The opponent in the small blind has raised to 200. I only need to call 100 more to see the flop in a 300-chip pot, giving me 3:1 odds. 8s 6s is a well-connected suited hand that plays well post-flop, making this a clear call to defend my big blind.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a board with an Ace. The Ace is generally better for the pre-flop raiser's range. I will check to the aggressor to see how they react. My hand is strong enough to check-call most standard continuation bets, but it's not strong enough to lead out into the raiser.

I hold middle pair (Sevens) with a King kicker on an Ace-high board (Ad 6c 7c). While I have reasonable showdown value, the Ace hits the Big Blind's defending range fairly hard. Betting here risks getting check-raised or called by better hands (Ax), and I likely won't get three streets of value. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs or call bets on safe turns. It is a classic pot-control spot with a marginal made hand in position.

I currently have middle pair (sixes) on a board of Ad-6c-7c-Ts. In the previous street (flop), the pre-flop aggressor checked back, which often indicates they lack a strong Ace or are playing a marginal hand cautiously. The Ten of Spades on the turn is a somewhat neutral card, though it completes some straight draws (like 89) and gives a higher pair to any Tx in my opponent's range. Since I am out of position and my hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet, I will check again to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap river or catch a bluff.

I hold a pair of Sevens with a King kicker on a board of Ad 6c 7c Ts. After checking back the flop, the turn brings a Ten, which is an overcard to my pair and connects with some of the Big Blind's defending range (Tx, straight draws like 89). While I still have showdown value beating 6x and missed draws, betting here is thin value and risks reopening the action to a check-raise. By checking back, I successfully control the pot size, get to see a free river card, and maintain my plan to get to showdown cheaply with a marginal made hand. This line protects my stack against stronger hands (Ax, Tx) while keeping the opponent's bluffing range wide on the river.

I have a pair of sixes on a board with several overcards (Ace, Queen, Ten). Since the pre-flop aggressor checked back both the flop and the turn, it's unlikely they have a strong hand like an Ace. However, my sixes are still weak and unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet. Checking is the best option to realize my showdown value and potentially catch a bluff or see a cheap showdown.

I hold a pair of Sevens on a board of Ad 6c 7c Ts Qd. The river Queen adds another overcard to my pair, making the board fairly wet with straight possibilities (KJ, 89) and three overcards to my pair (A, Q, T). The opponent has checked to me on all three streets (Flop, Turn, River). This line represents either a complete miss (missed clubs, low pairs) or a pot-control line with a marginal made hand (like a weak Ace or Ten). Betting here has little utility: worse hands like missed draws or a pair of Sixes will likely fold, and better hands like Ax, Tx, or Qx will likely call. My hand has enough showdown value to beat missed flush draws and lower pairs. Therefore, taking the free showdown is the highest EV play.

I am holding Ace-Queen suited, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Being on the button, I want to take the initiative and build a larger pot. A standard raise to 3 times the big blind is appropriate here to exert pressure and extract value from weaker hands in the Big Blind's range.

I hold 8s 2s in the Big Blind facing a 3x open raise (300 total) from the Button. My pot odds are roughly 33% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600). While 82s has some suited potential, it is one of the weakest hands in heads-up play. Being out of position against a larger-than-minimum raise makes it very difficult to realize enough equity to justify a call. Standard heads-up defense ranges usually fold this specific hand against a 3x open. Therefore, folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with J6o in a heads-up match. This is a marginal hand, but against an opponent who has shown tendencies to defend the Big Blind passively (calling and checking down) or fold, raising is profitable. J6o has reasonable equity to steal the blinds or play a small pot in position. Folding is too tight for heads-up play, and limping might invite aggression from an opponent who has been raising 3x on their button. A minimum raise to 200 puts pressure on the BB while risking a small amount.

I am in the Big Blind holding T2o. Pro (Button/SB) has raised to 200. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 100 to play for a 400 total pot), T2o is a very weak hand that realizes equity poorly out of position. Given the 150-hand duration of the match, there is no need to defend with the bottom of my range. I will fold and look for better spots.

In heads-up play, the small blind (button) should be opening a wide range of hands to put pressure on the big blind. Although 8-5 offsuit is a relatively weak hand, I have established a pattern of raising to 300 (3x) from the button, which has resulted in Pro folding in two of the three previous instances. I will maintain this aggressive posture to capitalize on the fold equity and pick up the blinds.

I hold 9h 4s (94o) in the Big Blind facing a 3bb raise (300 chips) from the Button. The pot odds required to call are 33% (200 to win 600). 94o is one of the weakest hands in heads-up poker, with poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. Although my raw equity against a wide opening range might be close to the required odds, my ability to realize that equity is low. Given the larger raise size (3bb vs min-raise) and the trash quality of the hand, the expected value of calling is negative. The prudent play is to fold.

I am on the Button (SB) with Q4s, a playable hand in heads-up poker due to its flush potential and high card value. The opponent has folded to my button raises 50% of the time (2 out of 4 hands) and has played passively post-flop when they do call. Raising allows me to potentially steal the blinds immediately or play a pot in position against a straightforward opponent. A min-raise to 200 is the standard sizing to achieve this risk/reward balance.

I am in the Big Blind with J7s. Pro has opened for a min-raise (2x). Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win a total pot of 300) and the strength of a suited Jack in heads-up play, this is a standard defend. J7s has enough equity against a wide button opening range and plays reasonably well post-flop.

I have flopped top pair with a 7 kicker. In heads-up play, top pair is a very strong hand. Since I am out of position as the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) to see if they continue with a bet. This allows me to keep their bluffing range in and control the size of the pot.

I hold a strong flush draw (Qh 4h) on a board of Js 3h 6h. The opponent checked to me. Given the opponent's history of passivity (checking down in previous hands), a continuation bet is effective here for two reasons: 1) It generates fold equity against random overcards or weak pairs that currently beat my Queen-high, and 2) It builds the pot for when I hit my flush. I have substantial equity (9 flush outs + 3 Queen outs), making this a high-value semi-bluff. A half-pot bet size is standard to apply pressure without over-committing.

I have flopped top pair with a 7 kicker. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Pro has made a standard half-pot continuation bet. While I want to protect against heart draws, raising here might isolate me against better Jacks (AJ, KJ, QJ) or overpairs. By calling, I keep Pro's bluffs and weaker hands in the pot, control the size of the pot, and can re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card (5h) is highly coordinated, completing a potential flush and opening straight possibilities. Pro has checked after betting the flop, which could indicate a realization of the board's danger or an attempt to trap. While I have top pair, my kicker is weak, and I do not hold a heart to block flushes. Checking back is the most sensible play to control the size of the pot and reach a showdown or re-evaluate on the river.

The turn card is the 5h, putting four hearts on the board (Js 3h 6h 5h). I have made a Queen-high flush with my Qh 4h. While this is a very strong hand (effectively the 3rd nuts), the four-flush board texture significantly changes the dynamic. Betting here is problematic for two reasons: 1) It folds out almost all hands that I beat (pairs, sets, straights without a heart), as they are drawing dead or very thin. 2) It isolates me against better flushes (Ah, Kh). If I bet and face a check-raise, I am in a miserable spot. Checking back is the superior line. It controls the pot size, avoids maximizing losses against the Nut or King flush, and under-represents my hand strength. This deception may induce the opponent to bluff on the river or call a value bet with a worse hand (like a lower flush or a set) that would have folded to a turn bet.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a board where the flush completed on the turn. After Pro checked back the turn, my hand retains some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to value bet for three streets or lead out into a potentially completed flush or straight. Checking is the best option to either reach a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from missed straight draws or lower pairs that Pro might have turned into a bluff.

Re-evaluating the board texture, I notice the previous analysis contained an error: the board is Js 3h 6h 5h 8s, which is a 3-flush board (Hearts), not 4-flush, as the Jack is a Spade. This significantly increases the value of my Queen-high flush (Qh 4h), as I now beat all straights, sets, and two-pairs, losing only to the Ace or King high flush. My check back on the turn has successfully under-represented my hand strength, likely leading the opponent to put me on a marginal hand like Top Pair or a missed straight draw. With the opponent checking to me on the river, their range appears capped and weak (likely Jx, a straight, or a small flush draw that missed/paired up). I must bet for value here. A half-pot bet of 400 is ideal: it extracts value from hands like KJ, QJ, AJ, or a straight (47, 24) that might hero-call thinking I am bluffing or betting thin, while not being so large that it folds out everything but the nuts.

On the river, the board is Js3h6h5h8s. I have top pair (Jacks) with a 7 kicker. Pro bet 400 into a pot of 800 (1/2 pot) after checking back the turn. The 5h on the turn completed the heart flush, and the 8s on the river doesn't change much. Pro's line (raise pre, bet flop, check turn, bet river) could represent a Jack with a better kicker (like AJ, KJ, QJ), a missed draw turned into a bluff, or perhaps a slow-played flush that decided to check the turn for pot control or to trap. Given that I have top pair in a heads-up match and the odds are 3:1 to call, I should call to see if my pair is good. Folding top pair here would be too exploitable, but raising is out of the question as only better hands would call.

I am holding 8c 3s on the button. This is a very weak hand, well below the threshold for a standard opening raise or even a limp-in. Given the stack sizes and the opponent's aggressive play in previous hands, I will fold this hand to avoid playing a weak range out of position later or putting chips into a pot with low equity.

I am on the Button with Queen-Nine suited (Qs9s), which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, typically ranked in the top 30% of hands. Given the heads-up dynamic and my previous history of raising from the button (which has been successful in taking down blinds or initiating action where I have position), a raise is the standard play here. I will min-raise to 200 to put pressure on the Big Blind, build the pot with a playable hand, and maintain the initiative. Folding is incorrect due to hand strength, and limping allows the opponent to see a flop for free with their entire range.
5h 6h is a strong suited connector hand, perfect for defending the big blind against a standard 2x open from the button. With 3:1 pot odds, this is a clear call to see a flop and realize my equity.

I have 6-high on an Ace-high board (9d 4s As) after calling a pre-flop raise from the Big Blind. This board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. My hand has very little equity and no real draws. My standard play here is to check to the aggressor and see how they react.

I hold Middle Pair (Nines) and a Queen-high Flush Draw on an A-9-4 two-tone board. This is a very high-equity hand. Betting serves multiple purposes: 1) Value: Getting called by worse pairs (4s, lower pocket pairs) or worse draws. 2) Protection: Denying equity to overcards (like King-high) that might otherwise realize their equity for free. 3) Building the pot: With such strong equity (pair + flush draw), I want to play a larger pot. If my opponent holds an Ace, I still have roughly 14 outs (9 flush outs, 2 nines, 3 queens) to improve. A standard continuation bet size of half-pot (200) balances value and bluff frequencies well on this texture.

I have 6-high on an Ace-high board with no significant draws. The pre-flop aggressor's range is much stronger on this texture, and a 1/2 pot continuation bet is expected from them. Given I have zero showdown value and almost no equity to improve, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

8h 4s is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation. While Pro has shown some vulnerability to pre-flop raises in previous hands, 84o lacks the post-flop playability to justify a raise or even a call. Conserving chips for better spots is more prudent here.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit [Js Ad] on the Button (SB). In heads-up play, this is a premium hand, well within the top percentage of starting hands. My strategy has been to consistently min-raise (to 200) from the Button to apply pressure and play pots in position. The opponent has defended their Big Blind by calling frequently but hasn't shown aggressive 3-betting tendencies against my opens yet. I will raise to 200 to build the pot for value while maintaining sizing consistency with my previous hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack of Clubs and Ten of Diamonds. The Pro on the Button has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more into a total pot of 300, giving me 3-to-1 odds on a call. JTo is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard button open, as it has good playability on many flops. I will call to see the flop and proceed from there.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit on a King-high board (8s-Kd-6s). I have missed this flop entirely, holding only two overcards to the middle of the board and no draws. As the Big Blind against the pre-flop raiser, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I will check and see if Pro decides to continue with a bet. If Pro bets, I will likely have to fold given the lack of equity and backdoors.

I hold AJo on a Kd 8s 6s board. While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop raiser (holding stronger Kings and Aces), this texture connects fairly well with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs, flush draws, straight draws). My hand has decent showdown value as Ace-high, and I hold the Js which gives me a backdoor nut flush draw and blocks some of the opponent's flush draws. Betting here folds out worse hands but risks getting check-raised, which would force me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity for free, and potentially catch a Jack, Ace, or Spade on the turn. It also protects my checking range. Given the opponent's passive tendencies shown in previous hands, checking is a solid play to get to showdown cheaply or delay c-bet on a safe turn.

On the turn (7h), the board is 8s-Kd-6s-7h. I currently hold Jack-high but have picked up a gutshot straight draw (any 9 gives me a straight). Pro checked back the flop, which suggests they likely don't have a strong King or a set. However, my hand still lacks showdown value. By checking, I can potentially see a free river card to hit my straight or evaluate based on Pro's turn action. If Pro checks behind again, it confirms their range is weak, and I might be able to take the pot on the river or win at showdown if they have total air.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit and am in position. The board is now Kd 8s 6s 7h. The 7h on the turn makes the board very coordinated, with potential straights (5-6-7-8 and 6-7-8-9 possibilities). My opponent has checked to me again. I checked the flop for pot control, and nothing on this turn card incentivizes me to start betting. My Ace-high still has some showdown value against missed flush draws or weaker air, but betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me (pairs, straights) or strong draws. By checking, I take a free card to try to hit an Ace or Jack on the river, or simply get to showdown cheaply with my Ace-high.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high (Jc Td). The board is 8s-Kd-6s-7h-2d. After calling a pre-flop raise, both the Pro and I have checked the flop and the turn. This suggests that the Pro does not have a strong hand (like a King or a high pair) or is playing very cautiously. However, my Jack-high has almost no showdown value and will likely lose to any pair or Ace-high. While I could attempt a bluff to take down the 400-chip pot, a missed gutshot on this board is not a very convincing story to represent. I will check and hope the Pro also checks so I can see if my Jack-high is somehow good, though I expect to lose this small pot if the Pro has any piece of the board or even Ace-high.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit (Ace-high) on a board of Kd 8s 6s 7h 2d. The action has gone check-check on the flop and turn. The opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to check-call or check-down with marginal hands in previous pots. My Ace-high has decent showdown value against missed spade draws (like QsTs, etc.) and weaker high cards. If I bet, I likely only fold out hands that I already beat (worse air) or chop with, while hands that beat me (any pair like a 2, 6, 7, or 8) will likely call a reasonable bet given the small pot size and my lack of aggression on previous streets. Therefore, checking back is the best option to realize my equity and potentially win at showdown without risking more chips.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces (Ah Ac), the strongest possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My goal is to extract maximum value from my opponent. Consistent with my previous opening raises from the button (Hands #2, #4, #6, and #8), I will raise to 300 to build the pot while maintaining a balanced representation of my range.

I am in the Big Blind with K8o facing a 3x open (300) from the Button (Flash). In heads-up play, K8o is a solid hand that is well above the median and has sufficient equity to defend against a wide button opening range. Folding is too tight given the 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 400). 3-betting is a viable alternative but can isolate me against a stronger range and bloat the pot while out of position. The most standard and prudent play is to call and see a flop.

I hold King-high (Kd8s) on a Q-9-6 rainbow flop. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. The board texture connects reasonably well with a typical button opening range (which includes many Qx and 9x hands). Leading out (donk betting) would not achieve much here, as better hands will call and worse hands might fold or float. I will check and evaluate based on my opponent's action.

I hold Pocket Aces, the strongest overpair on this 9h-6s-Qd board. Pro has checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. I need to bet for value against hands like top pair (Qx), middle pair (9x), or various straight draws (87, JT). A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard sizing to build the pot while keeping Pro's weaker range in the hand.

I hold King-high (Kd8s) on a Q-9-6 rainbow board and face a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600) from the Button. While I defended pre-flop, this flop texture favors the aggressor's range, which contains many Queens and Nines. I have no pair, no flush draw, and only a very weak runner-runner straight draw. Calling out of position with King-high hoping to hit a King or bluff catch later is negative EV without a specific read. The pot odds of 3:1 (25% equity required) are not sufficient given my hand's lack of playability and showdown value. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 2h, which is one of the worst starting hands in poker (unconnected, offsuit, low ranks). While I have been raising frequently from the button (100% so far in recorded hands), the opponent 'Flash' has defended their Big Blind by calling 5 out of 7 times (approx 71%). Against an opponent who defends this frequently, opening with the absolute bottom of my range is unprofitable as I will often be playing a bloated pot out of position with a hand that rarely flops well. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct decision here.

I have been dealt a pocket pair of threes (3c, 3h). This is a hand with good value in a heads-up situation, but it plays best by either taking the pot down pre-flop or hitting a set on the flop. Given I am on the button (SB), I will raise to 300 total (a standard 3x open) to build the pot and take control of the hand. This puts pressure on the opponent and allows me to represent strength.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 total) from the Button. My hand, Q9o, is well within the top 40% of hands and performs well against a typical wide heads-up button opening range. I need to call 200 more chips into a pot that will be 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds (requiring 25% equity). Q9o has approximately 50% equity against a standard opening range. While I will be out of position post-flop, folding here would be too tight, and 3-betting might be an overplay that isolates me against better hands. Therefore, defending by calling is the most standard and +EV play.

I am out of position on a dry, paired flop (3-4-3) with Queen-high. This board texture hits neither range particularly hard, but the pre-flop aggressor retains the range advantage with higher pockets and Ace-highs. Leading out (donk betting) with Q-high is unnecessary and turns my hand into a bluff prematurely. The standard play is to check to the aggressor to see if they continue with a c-bet or check back, allowing me to realize equity or bluff-catch later.

I have flopped quads (four of a kind), which is the absolute nuts on this board. Since the board is extremely dry (3-4-3) and I have blocked almost all possible combinations that could have hit my opponent, betting now is likely to result in a fold from any hand that didn't hit. By checking back, I allow the opponent to either bluff on the turn or potentially catch a pair or some equity that they might be willing to commit chips with later. I want to maximize value, and slow-playing here is the most effective way to do so.

On the turn 4c, the board now shows Ac-6d-5c-4c. My hand is two pair, Aces and Fives. I hold Top Pair with a Jack kicker on a board that now has three clubs. The 4c turn card completes potential flush draws. My opponent, Flash, has fired a second barrel of half-pot (1200). I have two pair (Aces and Fives) on a board that contains three clubs and several straight possibilities. I hold Top Pair with a Jack kicker (As Js) on a board of 5c 6d Ac 4c Qd. The pot is significant (4,800) after a pre-flop 3-bet and two barrels from Flash. Flash has now checked the river. I hold QhJd on a final board of 7s 5d Ad Td As. I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (Ah9s) on a board of [7c 5d 9c 5c]. The turn card (5c) is significant as it pairs the board and brings the third club, completing potential flushes. I hold A9o on a board of 7c 5d 9c 5c Ts. I have two pair (Nines and Fives) with an Ace kicker.
